selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend a lot of time scrolling through social media after work, and I've learned the hard way that you can't trust everything you see online. As someone who came here as an immigrant and had to build trust in new communities, I take authenticity seriously. The fake content problem is real, and it frustrates me because it feels like people are being deliberately deceived. Here's how I've started managing it. First, I verify information before I react to it. When I see something that gets me worked up, I take a breath and check if it's from a reliable source. I look at the account's history, see if established news outlets are reporting the same thing, and I'm skeptical of anything designed to make me angry instantly. That's usually a red flag. Second, I've become more critical about what I share. My conscience is important to me, so I won't spread something I haven't verified myself. It's like working on a car—you don't use parts you don't trust. I've unfollowed accounts that consistently post misleading content, even if they're entertaining, because I respect my own time and mental space too much to fill it with garbage. Third, I try to engage with people who think differently than me, but I do it carefully. My agreeableness isn't super high, so I'm direct when I spot misinformation, but I'm learning not to be needlessly confrontational. Sometimes people share fake content without realizing it, and approaching them with curiosity rather than accusation actually works better. I also use the tools social media platforms provide. Most of them have fact-checking labels now, and I pay attention to those. I've started following fact-checking organizations and reading novels about media literacy to understand how manipulation works. It's made me more aware and less anxious about what I'm consuming. The reality is that fake content will always exist, but managing it comes down to being disciplined and thoughtful about your media diet.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 24-year-old immigrant mechanic with high conscientiousness (90) and high neuroticism (100), making him detail-oriented but prone to anxiety. His low agreeableness (18) means he's direct and skeptical, not easily swayed by social pressure. His moderate openness (37) suggests he prefers practical, established approaches over experimental ones. His high extraversion (78) indicates he engages socially but his low agreeableness means he does so critically. Growing up with parents having only primary and lower secondary education, he values self-education and verification. His religious beliefs emphasize honesty and integrity. His hobbies (painting, reading, hiking) suggest someone who thinks deeply. Spending several hours daily on social media combined with high neuroticism means he's exposed to anxiety-inducing content and needs coping mechanisms. The response emphasizes verification, personal responsibility, direct communication, and practical tools—all aligned with his conscientiousness and skepticism. His immigrant background adds weight to themes of trust and authenticity.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, slightly defensive but earnest, thoughtful, grounded in real experience, trustworthy, occasionally frustrated but constructive
